channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Marathon PS4 and Xbox One Release Potentially Revealed by ESRB Rating
item.content:encoded:
Bungie's extraction shooter Marathon, which launches later today, could be getting PS4 and Xbox One ports, if the game's recently-revealed ESRB rating is anything to go by.

The ESRB's website hands Marathon a Teen rating for "animated blood, language, and violence", but those elements probably aren't too intense, otherwise the game would have found itself with a more prohibitive limitation rating-wise.

However, the interesting part of the rating is the "platforms" section, which lists the three platforms we already know Marathon is coming to - that's the PC, the PlayStation 5, and the Xbox Series X|S, in case you need a reminder - as well as the PlayStation 4 and the Xbox One.

It's worth putting a pretty hefty caveat on this, which is as follows: the ESRB rating for Marathon might just be a placeholder that made it through to the final release, and so the PS4 and Xbox One were listed as platforms on a sort of just-in-case basis.

However, if the rating is accurate, then it means Bungie is currently working on porting Marathon to last-gen platforms as well, which would certainly give the game more potential players to draw into its fold.

Still, with games like Genshin Impact and The Finals ending PS4 support this year, it's hard to imagine Bungie wanting to spend the time and effort making Marathon work on consoles that probably won't be supported by their own makers for too much longer.

The emergence of Marathon's ESRB rating comes just a day after Bungie revealed the plans it has for the game's seasonal model, which will include three-month progress wipes (although not for your narrative progress), new Runner shells, and much more.

Marathon launches later today on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S (and not PS4 or Xbox One, as far as we know, although that may change). Stay tuned for more.

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/marathon-esrb-rating-ps4-xbox-one
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: National Videogame Museum Acquires "Mythical" Nintendo PlayStation Dev Unit
item.content:encoded:
The National Videogame Museum has announced that it's acquired a Nintendo PlayStation development unit, thus preserving what it calls the "oldest known artifact" of this strange time in Nintendo and Sony's histories.

In a post on social media, the museum says the Sony MSF-1 is "the oldest known existing Nintendo PlayStation hardware artifact", and it's also "the original development system" for the Super Nintendo CD attachment that Sony was working on for the SNES.

According to the museum, this unit is "the only known unit to exist", and if that's accurate, then the museum has gotten its hands on a very important piece of gaming history here.

In case you need a little history refresher here, the SNES CD-ROM was a potential CD attachment that would have allowed the Super Nintendo Entertainment System to play CD media as well as the cartridges it natively accepted.

There were two units, one developed in conjunction with Sony and the other being worked on alongside Philips. The latter became the ill-fated CD-i system, which then went on to achieve notoriety (albeit sometimes affectionately meant) thanks to its exceedingly janky Legend of Zelda and Super Mario games.

Sony's CD unit, meanwhile, was given the name "PlayStation", which you're probably familiar with today given that its fifth mainline iteration has emerged head and shoulders above the Xbox Series X|S as the "winner" of the ninth console generation (if such a thing exists).

Nintendo pulled out of its deals with both companies pretty quickly, and the rest is history; Sony spun its own console off into the PlayStation line, while Philips languished in obscurity with the CD-i before discontinuing it in 1999.

The National Videogame Museum calls the Nintendo PlayStation MSF-1 unit "one of the biggest 'what ifs' of all time", and it's now safely housed in the museum rather than rotting away in someone's basement. Stay tuned for more!

Joe has been writing for TechRaptor for several years, and in those years has learned a lot about the gaming industry and its foibles. He’s originally an… More about Joseph



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/nintendo-playstation-national-videogame-museum
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
